steps.n: Connected the letters together and added vowel marks to show how it's pronounced
Added double-damma to show this is the main subject doing the action in the sentence. It's like saying 'Woe is upon...'

steps.m: to give less than what is due by cheating in measurement
steps.n: This root refers to giving people less than they deserve, like cheating in measurements

steps.m: to repeatedly give less than due measure
steps.n: The doubled 'f' shows this is done repeatedly or as a habit
Added مُ at start and changed vowels to show this describes someone who does this action
Added ين to show this refers to many people who do this

steps.m: the ones who cheat in measurement
steps.n: Added 'the' to make it specific/definite
This is the basic form of the word used to point to someone or something. It's a special word that doesn't come from a root.
Added ال at the beginning and doubled the ذ letter to make it stronger. This makes it point to something specific.

steps.m: those who (referring to the cheaters in measuring mentioned later)
steps.n: Added ن at the end to make it talk about many people instead of just one. This word points forward to the cheaters mentioned in the next part of the verse.
This is the basic form of the word that means 'when' or 'at the time'. It's used to talk about specific times.

steps.m: when (introducing a condition in this ayah about people who take full measure for themselves)
steps.n: Adding ا at the end makes it talk about conditions or things that might happen in the future, rather than specific past times. Here it introduces what happens when people take measurements from others.

steps.m: to measure (something by weight or volume)

steps.n: The root letters were connected with vowels. The middle letter ي became ا to make it easier to say, which is common in Arabic verbs. This makes it mean 'he measured'.

steps.m: he took a measurement for himself
steps.n: Added اِ at the beginning and ت after first letter to show doing the action for oneself

steps.m: they took measurements for themselves (from others)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show multiple people did this action. In this ayah, it refers to those who demand full measure when receiving from others.

steps.m: they took measurements for themselves (from others)
steps.n: Changed the initial اِ to ٱ, which is just a different way of writing the same sound. Added the small circle (۟) which is just a pronunciation mark.

steps.m: against (related to يَسْتَوْفُونَ - they take in full)
steps.n: This is a preposition that shows something is being done against or at the expense of someone else. In this ayah, it shows that the measuring/taking in full is being done against or at the expense of people (ٱلنَّاسِ).

steps.m: to be social or friendly with others

steps.n: Changed the root into a noun meaning 'people' by adding a long 'aa' sound
Added a doubling mark to emphasize the 'n' sound
Added ال to specify these particular people
Added kasra at the end to show this word is being acted upon by something before it in the sentence
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Added يَسْتَ to show someone is demanding or seeking to get their full rights/dues. The ي at the start shows it's happening now (present tense).
Added ون at the end to show multiple people are doing the action

steps.m: when (introduces a condition that happens repeatedly)
steps.n: إِذَا is a conditional particle that introduces situations that commonly occur or are expected to happen. In this ayah, it introduces what happens when people measure or weigh things.

steps.m: and when (connects this condition to previous statements)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this condition with what came before it. This shows this is part of a series of related actions or situations.

steps.m: to measure out (especially grains or liquids)

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
The middle letter ي changes to ا because it's a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter)
Added وا to show that a group of people did the action (related to وَزَنُوهُمْ - 'they weighed for them' which comes next in the ayah)

steps.m: they measured out for them (refers to giving less in measure to customers)
steps.n: Added هُمْ to show who received the action - refers to customers being cheated in measuring, as explained by the context of the surah
This is a connecting word that joins two similar actions (measuring and weighing) in this verse. It shows these are two different ways the dishonest people cheat others.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed the ending to show multiple people doing the action
Added هُمْ at the end to show who was weighed
Added وَّ at beginning to mean 'and', with emphasis shown by the shadda
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a past tense verb
Added يُ at start to make it present tense and changed the pattern to make it mean 'causes someone to lose' instead of just 'lost'

steps.m: they make (others) lose (when measuring for them)
steps.n: Added plural ending to show multiple people doing the action. In this ayah, it refers to those who give less in measurement to others.

This is an attention-grabbing word that introduces an important statement and shows the speaker wants to correct a wrong idea. Here it introduces and emphasizes that these people are wrong in what they think.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
The last ن gets doubled with a shadda to make the meaning stronger
Added يَ at start and changed vowels to show it's happening now. This word shows what those who deny the Day of Judgment are thinking.
This is the basic form of the word that points to multiple things or people
Adding ا shows that we're pointing to something far away
Adding ئِ specifies we're talking about people or things

steps.m: those people over there (subject of يَظُنُّ)
steps.n: Adding كَ at the end makes it more specific about the distance and connects it as the subject of يَظُنُّ (they think) in this ayah
This particle introduces what is being thought, connecting to the verb يَظُنُّ (they think) in the ayah

steps.m: that they (the ones being thought about)
steps.n: Added هُم to show who is being referred to - in this case, it refers back to أُو۟لٰٓئِكَ (those people) from earlier in the ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to passive participle showing who receives the action of being sent forth/resurrected
Added plural ending to show it applies to multiple people

steps.m: those who are DEFINITELY sent forth/resurrected
steps.n: The doubled م makes the meaning stronger and more emphatic
Connected the root letters together with vowel marks to make the basic noun meaning 'day'

steps.m: for a day (connected to عَظِيمٍ - great)
steps.n: The letter ل (li) was added at the start to mean 'for', and the tanween (double dots under) shows it's indefinite - like saying 'a day' instead of 'the day'
The ي between the second and third root letters makes this an intensive adjective, showing this is a truly mighty or magnificent day

steps.m: mighty/magnificent (describing يَوْمٍ, in a possessive/connected state)
steps.n: The tanween kasra (ٍ) shows this is describing an indefinite noun (يَوْمٍ - a day) and is connected to it grammatically
Connected the letters together with vowel marks. This word tells when the standing happens in يَقُومُ (stands).
The fatha at the end shows this word answers 'when?' about the standing. It's like saying 'on the Day' or 'at the time when'.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense form

Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels. The و becomes long because it's between a damma and sukun. This verb describes what happens on the Day (يَوْمَ) mentioned in the ayah.
The ُ at the end shows this is a complete present tense verb in its basic case
Changes from verb (being social) to noun (people/humans)
Added 'the' to show we're talking about all people who will stand on that day. This word is the subject of يَقُومُ (stands/rises) in this ayah.
Added ُ (damma) at the end to show this word is doing the action of standing/rising

steps.m: master/lord (one who nurtures and takes care of others)
steps.n: Doubling the middle letter shows this is someone who does this action perfectly. A رَبّ is someone who takes complete care of others.
Added kasra at the end because this word is connected to 'the worlds' that comes after it, showing it owns or controls the worlds

steps.m: for/to the master/lord (being described by الْعٰلَمِينَ)
steps.n: Added لِ at beginning to show this is who the people are standing before/for in يَقُومُ ٱلنَّاسُ (the people stand)
Changes the concept of knowing into the thing that is known (the world/universe that can be known)
Makes the word plural by adding ين, showing we're talking about multiple worlds or realms
Adding ال makes it specific - these particular worlds. The َ at the end shows it belongs to رَبِّ (Lord) in the phrase رَبِّ ٱلْعٰلَمِينَ (Lord of the worlds)
This is the basic form of the word that means to stop or prevent something

steps.m: No indeed! (strongly rejecting what was mentioned before)
steps.n: Adding the long alif at the end makes this word stronger and more emphatic. It's used at the beginning of statements to strongly reject what came before.
This is the basic form of the word that shows something might happen or is uncertain
Adding the shadda makes the word stronger and changes it from showing uncertainty to showing certainty. It emphasizes that the record of the wicked (كِتٰبَ ٱلْفُجَّارِ) is definitely in sijjeen.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed the vowel pattern and added alif to create a noun meaning the result of writing
Added fatha at the end because this word comes after إِنَّ which makes the noun after it take fatha

steps.m: to break bounds/limits, to act wickedly

steps.n: Added fathas to connect the letters and make it a past tense verb
Changed pattern to show someone who does the action
Changed to broken plural pattern فُعَّال to show many wicked people
Added ال to specify these particular wicked people

steps.m: of the wicked people (possessed by كِتٰبَ)
steps.n: Added kasra to show this describes whose book it is (the record of the wicked people)
This is the basic preposition meaning 'in' or 'within'

steps.m: surely in/within (emphasizes that the record of the wicked is in سِجِّينٍ)
steps.n: Added لَ at the beginning to add emphasis, like saying 'definitely' or 'surely'. This emphasizes that the record is definitely in سِجِّينٍ.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
The extra ي and kasra vowels make the meaning stronger

steps.m: a deep prison (being described in كِتٰبَ الفُجَّارِ)
steps.n: Added tanween (the -in sound) to show this is a deep, dark prison where evil deeds are kept
This is a question word that asks about things (like 'what' in English)
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this word with what came before it
The regular alif is written as a long alif (آ) to show emphasis in the question. This word is asking about the word سِجِّينٌ that comes later in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Transforms from basic 'to know' into causative 'to make know/inform'
Added alif maksura (ى with a small alif above it) at the end to show this is a weak verb

steps.m: made you know (وَمَآ before it makes this a question: 'and what made you know?')
steps.n: Added the suffix كَ meaning 'you' as the object (the one being made to know). This word follows وَمَآ which makes it into a question.
This is an interrogative particle asking 'what?' It's being used as the object of the verb أَدْرَىٰكَ (made you know/informed you) in this ayah, asking 'and what made you know?'
Basic meaning of imprisoning or confining
Added kasra vowels and doubled the middle letter to create an intensive noun form that refers to a specific type of record
Added tanween dammah to make it indefinite (a record)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he wrote' to 'something written' by changing the middle vowel to a long aa sound
Added two damma marks to show this is 'a' book (not 'the' book)
The alif (ا) was written as a long alif (ٰ). The tanween dammah (ٌ) shows this is a subject being described by مَّرْقُومٌ (inscribed/written)
Connected the root letters with fatha to form basic past tense verb
Changed to passive participle showing something that has been marked or numbered
Added shadda on initial م to emphasize the meaning
Added tanween dammah to show this is describing the book (كِتٰبٌ) mentioned before it

steps.m: woe/destruction (threatening those who deny in يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ)
steps.n: Added vowel marks to show pronunciation. This word expresses a severe warning or threat of punishment to those who deny the truth.

steps.m: woe/destruction! (threatening those who deny in يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ)
steps.n: Added a double-damma mark (tanween) to show this is a strong warning or threat, like saying 'Watch out!'
Connected the letters with vowel marks to make it a noun. The و keeps its sukun because it's a weak letter.

steps.m: on that day (followed by لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ - for the deniers)
steps.n: Added ئِذٍ to specify 'that particular day' being discussed. This word connects to what comes after it (لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ) to say 'on that day, for the deniers'
This is the basic form of lying
Adding مُ at beginning and ِ on middle letter shows who does the action of denying
Doubling the middle letter shows this person denies things again and again
The ين ending makes it refer to multiple people who deny
Added definite article ال to specify particular deniers

steps.m: for/to the ones who repeatedly deny
steps.n: Adds preposition 'for/to' with assimilated laam
This is a relative pronoun used to refer to a group of people. It's a particle that doesn't come from a root.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
The doubled middle letter makes the meaning stronger - it's not just lying, but strongly denying or rejecting
Changes from past tense (he denied) to present tense (he denies) by adding يُ at the start

steps.m: they strongly deny/reject (referring to those who deny the Day of Judgment)
steps.n: The ون ending shows this is about multiple people. This word connects to الَّذِينَ (those who) before it and بِيَوْمِ الدِّينِ (the Day of Judgment) after it.
Connected the root letters with fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create the noun 'day'
Adding kasra at the end shows this word is possessed by or connected to الدِّينِ (the judgment/religion)
Added بِ at the beginning to show this is happening by/in/with the day
Connected the root letters and added kasra to show it's a noun

steps.m: the judgment/religion (object of بِيَوْمِ - Day of)
steps.n: Added 'the' (ال) at the start to make it specific, doubled the د sound, and has an 'i' sound at the end because it follows the word 'Day of'
This is a particle that can function as either a question word or a relative pronoun
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it. This word is the subject (doer) of the verb يُكَذِّبُ (denies) in this ayah.

steps.m: to deny or declare something false

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Added shadda to middle letter to show intensity of the action
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing middle vowel to kasra. This word comes after مَا (not) in the ayah, showing that someone does not deny.
Basic preposition that shows how something is done

steps.m: with it/by it (acted on by يُكَذِّبُ)
steps.n: Added pronoun suffix meaning 'it/him'. This word is connected to the verb يُكَذِّبُ (denies/rejects) to show what is being denied.

steps.m: with it/by it (acted on by يُكَذِّبُ)
steps.n: Added pronunciation helpers (ۦٓ) that don't change the meaning
Basic particle form that shows exception - it excludes what comes after it from what came before. In this ayah, it shows that only a specific type of person (every transgressor, sinful) denies.
Added alif at the end to help connect with the next word. This particle introduces the exception 'every transgressor, sinful' in the ayah.
Adding damma vowels and shadda creates a word meaning 'every' or 'all' that describes the word أَثِيمٍ (sinner) in this ayah

steps.m: to go beyond proper limits/to transgress against others

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Added اِعْتَ to show someone chose to go beyond limits/boundaries on purpose
Changed to active participle form by adding مُ at start and tanwin kasra at end. This describes someone who does this action habitually.
Added ي between second and third root letters and vowel marks to create intensive form showing someone who repeatedly commits sin
Added tanwin kasra to show this word describes the transgressor mentioned earlier in the ayah

steps.m: when (referring to something that will happen in the future, followed by تُتْلَىٰ/are recited)
steps.n: This is a time word that shows when something happens. In this ayah, it introduces what happens when verses are recited to someone.

steps.m: to follow in sequence or recite
steps.n: Basic meaning of following something or reciting
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb meaning 'he recited'. The و at the end becomes ا, which is common in Arabic verbs.
Changed vowels to make it passive voice (something being done to it). The ا changes to ي in passive form.

steps.m: it is being recited (to عَلَيْهِ - to him)
steps.n: Changes from past tense 'was recited' to present tense 'is being recited'. The ى at the end shows it's happening to/on something.

steps.m: it is being recited (to عَلَيْهِ - to him)
steps.n: Added the small alif (ٰ) above the ى to show it's being stretched in pronunciation
Basic preposition meaning 'upon' or 'on'
Changed ى to يْ to prepare for attaching the pronoun
Added هِ at the end to mean 'him'. This word shows who our verses are being recited to.
Root meaning relates to something being clear or evident
The fatha vowel helps form the singular noun 'sign/verse'
Adding ُ shows this word is in the subject/nominative case
Adding نا shows these signs belong to us (Allah)

steps.m: our signs, our verses (that are being recited)
steps.n: Adds 'our' and shows these are the signs being recited in the verse
Basic meaning of speaking or saying
Added fatha marks to show this happened in the past and was done by 'he'
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter. This word is connected to أَسٰطِيرُ (tales) which comes after it in the ayah, showing what was said.

steps.m: to write in lines, to inscribe
steps.n: Root relates to writing or drawing lines
Added vowels (fatha) to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he wrote'
Changed from a verb (writing) to a noun (the written thing) by adding long 'aa' sound and changing first vowel
Added 'أ' at start and changed middle vowels to make it mean 'one legend/story' instead of just 'writing'
Changed to plural form to show many stories/legends
Added final damma to show this word is the subject of قَالَ (he said). The alif is written as a long alif (ٰ) in this form.
Root meaning relates to being first or preceding others
Added vowels and doubled the middle letter to make it mean 'first one'
Added ين to make it plural. The ِ shows it's owned by the word أَسٰطِيرُ (tales/stories) in this ayah
Added ال to make it specific - referring to particular ancient people rather than any ancient people
This is the base form of the word that means a simple negation
Adding shadda makes the negation stronger

steps.m: No indeed! (strongly rejecting what came before)
steps.n: Adding alif at the end makes it an emphatic particle that strongly rejects what was mentioned before. In this ayah, it's rejecting the false beliefs of the disbelievers mentioned in previous verses.
This is a particle that strongly disagrees with what came before (كَلَّا - 'No!') and introduces the real reason. It's like saying 'Actually, the truth is...'

steps.m: to rust or cover something until it becomes corrupt

steps.n: Added fathas to connect the letters and make it a past tense verb
The ي changes to ا because it's a hollow verb (has weak middle letter)
Basic preposition showing something being on top of or over something else. In this ayah, it connects to 'their hearts' to show what has been covered.
Added the small alif (ٰ) at the end to show this is a complete word, not connected to what follows
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'heart'. This word is being acted upon by رَانَ (covers/seals) in this ayah.
Changed the vowels and added و to make the word mean more than one heart
Added the plural masculine possessive pronoun 'their'
Basic form of the relative pronoun that refers to actions or things. Here it refers to the actions that follow (يَكْسِبُونَ - they used to earn).

steps.m: what (they) (connects to كَانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ - they used to earn)
steps.n: Added a shadda (doubling mark) to help the word flow better when reading. Shows this word connects to 'they used to earn' later in the sentence.
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter in the past tense
Added وا at the end to show it's 'they' (for a group). This word is the subject of يَكْسِبُونَ (they earn) in this ayah.

steps.m: to gain or earn something through effort

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense verb
Added يَ at beginning to show present tense, changed vowels to show ongoing action

steps.m: they are earning/gaining (referring to the disbelievers mentioned earlier in the ayah)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people doing the action. This refers back to the disbelievers mentioned earlier in the verse.
This is the basic negation word that means 'no'
Adding ا at the end makes the negation stronger

steps.m: No indeed! (strongly rejecting what was mentioned before)
steps.n: The آ is a spelling variation of ا that shows the sound is stretched when reading, making the rejection even stronger
This is a special word that makes what comes after it stronger or more certain, like adding 'definitely' in English

steps.m: indeed they (are blocked from their Lord that Day)
steps.n: Added هُمْ which means 'they/them'. This connects to the rest of the ayah which tells us they are blocked from their Lord.

steps.m: away from (shows separation from رَّبِّهِمْ - their Lord)
steps.n: This is a preposition that shows separation or distance from something. In this ayah, it shows that people are being blocked away from their Lord.

steps.m: to nurture and take care of something completely

steps.n: The doubled letter shows complete/intense nurturing
The word رَبّ (lord) gets هِمْ added at the end to mean 'their lord'. The ِ (kasra) under ب helps connect these smoothly. The ّ (shadda) shows emphasis.
Added vowel marks to make the basic noun meaning 'day'
Added fatha at the end to show this word is being used as a time expression

steps.m: on that day (when they will be blocked from their Lord)
steps.n: Added ئِذٍ to specify 'that specific day' being discussed in the ayah. This refers to the Day of Judgment when, as mentioned in the ayah, people will be blocked from seeing their Lord.

steps.m: to block or prevent from seeing/reaching

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Changes to show the state of being blocked rather than the action of blocking
Makes it refer to a group of people who are blocked
Adds emphasis to show this is definitely true

steps.m: surely they are blocked/prevented (from their Lord on that Day)
steps.n: The laam letter gets a shadda (doubled) to make the word stronger, showing how firmly they are blocked from seeing Allah

steps.m: then (connects to إِنَّهُمْ لَصَالُوا۟ ٱلْجَحِيمِ - they will burn in Hellfire)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that shows something happens after a delay or with importance. Here it connects to what happens next - that they will enter Hellfire.
This is a special word that makes what comes after it stronger and more certain

steps.m: indeed they (subject of لَصَالُوا 'they will burn')
steps.n: Added هُمْ to show we're talking about a group. This word connects to لَصَالُوا which tells us what they will do (burn).
Root letters joined together with fatha marks to make past tense 'he' form. The ي at the end becomes ى when it has fatha.
Changed to plural 'they' by adding وا at the end, and added ا in middle to show future tense

steps.m: surely they will burn/enter fire (object: الْجَحِيمِ - the Hellfire)
steps.n: Added لَ at beginning to emphasize that this will definitely happen. This word is connected to الْجَحِيمِ (the Hellfire) which shows what they will enter.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Pattern فَعِيل adds intensity to base meaning

steps.m: the hellfire (being described in this ayah)
steps.n: The ٱلْ makes it specific ('the' hellfire), and the ِ at the end shows it's being described or possessed by something
This is the basic form of the word that shows something happens after something else

steps.m: then (connects to يُقَالُ - it will be said)
steps.n: The word 'then' gets dots on top to show it connects one thing to another, like connecting two parts of a story
Basic meaning of speaking or saying something
The root letters were given vowels to make 'he said' in the past tense. The middle letter و became ا, which is common for this type of word.

Added يُ at the beginning and ُ at the end to make it passive present tense. This shows that something is being said without specifying who is saying it. In this ayah, it introduces what will be said.
This is the basic attention-drawing word that starts many pointing words in Arabic

steps.m: this (being pointed to in يُقَالُ - it is said)
steps.n: Added ذا which makes it point to something specific and nearby. The small alif (ٰ) shows this is being used to point to something. In this ayah, it's pointing to what is being said.
Starting with the basic demonstrative word that points to things

steps.m: who/which/that (referring to 'this' that you were told about)
steps.n: Adding ٱلّ changes it from pointing to things we can see to connecting parts of a sentence by referring back to something mentioned before
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
The middle weak letter و drops out, and the vowels change to match the pattern for hollow verbs (verbs with و or ي in the middle)
Added تُ at the end to show 'you' did the action in the past
Added م to make it plural 'you all'. This word refers back to the people being addressed in this ayah.
This is a basic preposition that shows how something is done
Added هِ which means 'it' to show what the preposition applies to
Added small ي (ۦ) after هِ as a pronunciation helper. This word is what you are denying/rejecting in تُكَذِّبُونَ (you deny/reject).

steps.m: to deny, to reject as false
steps.n: Basic meaning of lying or being false
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense verb
The doubled middle letter makes the meaning stronger - showing complete or repeated rejection
Changed to present tense with 'you' as subject

steps.m: you (all) deny intensely/repeatedly (the truth that was just mentioned)
steps.n: Added plural marker to show multiple people are doing the action. This refers back to the previous rejection mentioned in the ayah.
Final vowel mark added to complete the present tense plural form
This is a particle of emphatic negation and deterrence. It's stronger than just saying 'no'
Adding ا at the end makes the word stronger, like adding 'indeed' in English

steps.m: No indeed! (strongly rejecting what was mentioned before)
steps.n: The long alif (آ) at the end shows extra emphasis, like when you want to strongly disagree with something
This particle is used at the start of sentences to make them stronger and more certain
Adding the shadda (ّ) and kasra (ِ) makes the word stronger. This word emphasizes that what comes after it (كِتٰبَ ٱلْأَبْرَارِ - the record of the righteous) is definitely true.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed the pattern to make a noun that represents the result of writing
The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) and fatha is added at the end because this word is the object of إِنَّ (indeed/verily)
Added kasra at the end because this word comes after في in the ayah
Makes the word definite
Starting with the emphasis particle ل with a fatha to show strong confirmation
Added the preposition فِ (in) with a kasra to show location or state
Added ى at the end to complete the word. This preposition connects to the word عِلِّيِّينَ (highest places) in the ayah, showing where the record is located.
This root carries the basic meaning of being high or elevated
Adding يّ makes it into a special name/description
The ينَ ending makes this a plural noun referring to the highest places in heaven
This is an interrogative particle used to ask questions
Added و at the beginning to connect this question with what comes after it. This word connects to أَدْرَىٰكَ (made you know) to ask 'and what made you know?'
The alif is written with a small alif above it (آ) to show it's pronounced longer for emphasis
Basic meaning of knowing or understanding something
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create past tense verb
Added أَ at beginning with sukun on د to make it causative - showing someone causing knowledge in someone else
Added alif maksura (ى with small alif above) because it's at the end of a word
Added كَ at the end to show 'you' as the object (the one being made to know). This word follows وَمَآ (and what) in the ayah.
This is a question word used to ask 'what is something?' In this ayah, it's asking 'what is عِلِّيُّونَ?'
Basic meaning of height or elevation
Doubling the middle letter makes the meaning stronger - very high/elevated
Adding ون makes it refer to multiple high places or levels

steps.m: the highest ones (referring to the record books of the righteous)
steps.n: Added ون to show this refers to multiple things. In this ayah, it specifically refers to the highest places where the records of righteous people are kept.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
The verb 'he wrote' changes to mean 'a book' by changing the vowels and adding an alif - turning an action into the thing that gets written
Adding tanween makes the noun indefinite. This word is in nominative case and is described by the adjective مَّرْقُومٌ (inscribed) which follows it.

steps.m: something that is written/recorded (passive participle of كِتٰبٌ)
steps.n: Changed the word to show it's receiving the action of writing/recording. This word describes كِتٰبٌ (a book/record) in this ayah.
The doubled letter makes the meaning stronger, showing it's definitely/surely written
Added tanween dammah to show this is describing كِتٰبٌ (a book/record) in a general way
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the past tense form
Adding ي at the start shows this is happening now (present tense)

steps.m: he witnesses it (the record book that المقربون witness)
steps.n: Adding هُ at the end shows what is being witnessed. This word is connected to المقربون (those brought near) who are doing the witnessing.
Added the vowels 'a' and 'u' to make it mean 'he became close' in the past
Adding مُ at start and doubling ر shows this is someone who has been made close (not someone who became close on their own)
Adding ون makes it plural (more than two)

steps.m: the ones who are brought near (witnesses of يَشْهَدُهُ)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it specific. These are the witnesses mentioned in يَشْهَدُهُ (witness it)
This is the basic form of the word that means 'if' or 'whether'
Adding shadda changes this from 'if' to a word that emphasizes what comes after it. Here it emphasizes that the righteous (ٱلْأَبْرَارَ) are definitely in pleasure.

steps.m: to be righteous, to treat with kindness
steps.n: This is the basic meaning that all words from this root share
The doubled ر shows this is a strong quality in someone's character
This form أَفْعَال is a common way to make plurals in Arabic. The أ at the start and ا after the second letter work together to show multiple people
Added ال to make it specific and َ at the end because it comes after إِنَّ (indeed/truly) in the ayah
This is a basic preposition showing that the righteous (ٱلْأَبْرَارَ) are in pleasure (نَعِيمٍ)
The لَ adds emphasis because this comes after إِنَّ (indeed), making the statement even stronger - like saying 'truly' or 'certainly'. This emphasizes that the righteous are definitely in pleasure.

steps.m: to be blessed with comfort and happiness
steps.n: This root is about comfort, pleasure and good living
The ي makes it describe a strong, lasting feeling of happiness
Shows this paradise belongs to or is for the righteous ones (الْأَبْرَارَ) mentioned in the ayah
This is the basic form showing the concept of being high or elevated
Adding ى at the end turns this into a preposition showing position. In this ayah, it shows the believers are reclining upon thrones while looking (يَنظُرُونَ).
This is the basic concept of the root
Root أرك became a noun by adding ي and ة to make 'one couch/throne' you can recline on
Changed from single couch (areekah) to plural couches (araa'ik) - like changing 'chair' to 'chairs'
Added ال to make it specific
Added kasra at the end because it follows عَلَى (upon)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing middle vowel to ُ

steps.m: they look/watch (referring to the blessed people on thrones mentioned in عَلَى ٱلْأَرَآئِكِ)
steps.n: Added ونَ to show multiple people (3 or more males) are doing the action. In this ayah, it refers to the blessed people mentioned as sitting on thrones (عَلَى ٱلْأَرَآئِكِ)
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changes from past to present tense and adds 'you' or 'she' as the doer
This is a basic preposition that shows where you can recognize something - in this case, where you can see the brightness of blessing (on their faces)
The ي changes to ى when it comes at the end of a word - this is just a writing rule and doesn't change the meaning
Connected the letters together and added vowel marks to make the basic singular noun
Changed the vowels and added و to make it plural, showing many faces
Added ِ to connect it to the next part that shows possession
Added هِمْ which means 'their'. This word is what is being recognized/known in the phrase 'you will recognize in their faces'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make it a past-tense verb

steps.m: radiance/brightness (that you see on وُجُوهِهِمْ their faces)
steps.n: Changed to a noun meaning the quality or state of being bright and radiant. This radiance is what is seen on their faces.

steps.m: to be comfortable, to live in ease and blessing

steps.n: Added ي between second and third root letters to show intense quality, like saying 'great blessing' or 'complete comfort'
Added ٱل to make it specific 'the bliss', doubled the ن with shadda for emphasis, and added kasra at the end because this word is connected to what comes before it in the ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha marks. The final ي changes to ى when it comes at the end of a word.
Added يُ at start and changed vowels to show passive voice - the person receives the action instead of doing it
Added وْنَ to show multiple people receive the action. This word connects to رَّحِيقٍ (pure sealed wine) which is what they are given to drink.
Basic form of the preposition showing where something comes from
Added kasra (ِ) under م to show how to pronounce it correctly
Added vowels and ي to make it mean a specific pure drink (wine)
Added shadda to emphasize the purity

steps.m: of pure wine (object of يُسْقَوْنَ - they are given to drink)
steps.n: Added -in ending to show this is the wine they are drinking
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed to show something that received the action of sealing
Added emphasis by doubling the م sound at the start
Added tanween kasra to show this describes what kind of رَّحِيقٍ (pure drink) is being talked about
Changed the vowel pattern and added alif to make it mean the tool or thing used for sealing
The word changed to show it's the doer in 'its seal is musk'. The middle letter got longer.
Added هُۥ meaning 'its' to show possession. This complete word is the subject in the sentence 'its seal is musk', referring to the seal on the special drink mentioned earlier.

steps.m: to hold, grasp, or emit fragrance

steps.n: Forms a noun for the fragrant substance by adding kasra on first letter and sukun on middle letter. This word refers to the seal or final taste mentioned in the previous word خِتٰمُهُۥ (its seal).
Added double damma to show this is the main subject being described
This is a preposition that shows location or containment
Added و with fatha (وَ) at the beginning to connect this word to the previous word مِسْكٌ (musk). This shows that something is 'in' addition to the musk mentioned before.
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun meaning 'this' for pointing to something nearby
The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) to show distance
Adding لِ makes it point to something further away
Adding كَ makes it point to a specific thing being talked about
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
Form VI shows people competing against each other, like in a race or contest
Changed from command form to 'he is competing' (present tense)
Added lam of purpose/command to indicate 'let/should'

steps.m: so let him compete with others
steps.n: Added فَ to connect with previous statement

steps.m: so let him compete with others
steps.n: Adds the jussive mood marker to show this is a command

steps.m: to compete, to strive for something valuable

steps.n: Added تَ at the beginning and ا after first root letter to show people competing against each other

steps.m: those who compete with each other
steps.n: Added مُ at the start to change from the action (competing) to the people doing the competing

steps.m: those who compete with each other (plural men)
steps.n: Added ون to show we're talking about multiple men

steps.m: the ones who compete with each other (plural men)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ to specify these particular competitors
Added vowel marks to make a past tense verb meaning 'he mixed'
Changes the verb into a noun meaning the result of mixing. The ا after ز and the pattern change from مَزَج to مِزَاج creates a noun for the result.
Added هُ to show possession (his) and ُ (damma) to show this is the subject doing something in the sentence.
The وَ means 'and', connecting this to the previous phrase
This is a basic preposition that shows the drink mentioned before (وَمِزَاجُهُۥ) comes from or is mixed with تَسْنِيمٍ (a special spring in Paradise)

steps.m: to rise up, be elevated, or reach a peak

steps.n: Forms a proper noun for a special spring in Paradise that flows from above. The name comes from how it flows down from a high place.
The ٍ shows this word comes after مِن (from/of) in the ayah. It's like saying 'from a spring called Tasneem'
Connected the root letters together to form a noun meaning a source or spring of water

steps.m: a spring (object of يَشْرَبُ - drinks from)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to show this is what is being drunk from. This spring is what the special people (ٱلْمُقَرَّبُونَ) drink from.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the past tense verb
Adds nominative case ending
This is a preposition that shows how something is done
Added the feminine pronoun ها to show this refers to the spring (عَيْنًا) mentioned earlier in the verse that the close ones drink from
Adding مُ at the beginning changes it from 'to be near' to 'someone who is brought near (by another)'

steps.m: one who is drawn especially near
steps.n: Adding shadda (ّ) on middle letter shows this is done with special care or emphasis

steps.m: those who are drawn especially near
steps.n: The ون ending makes it plural (more than one) and shows they are the ones doing or receiving the main action in the sentence

steps.m: the ones who are drawn especially near (referring to special ranks of angels and righteous people)
steps.n: The ال prefix shows we're talking about a specific, known group. In Islamic context, this refers to those given special nearness to Allah.
Basic emphasis word that makes statements stronger

steps.m: indeed/verily (emphasizes that ٱلَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا۟ were mocking)
steps.n: Adding shadda makes the word stronger and changes it from meaning 'if' to definitely meaning 'indeed/verily'. In this ayah, it emphasizes that those who committed crimes were definitely mocking the believers.
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific. The shadda on ل shows it combines with the following letter.

steps.m: those who (referring to the criminals mentioned in أَجْرَمُوا۟)
steps.n: Added ين at the end to show we're talking about multiple people. This word connects to أَجْرَمُوا۟ (they committed crimes) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Adding أَ at the beginning changes the meaning to show entering into a state of being criminal
The وا shows a group of people (all male or mixed) did this action. This word connects to كَانُوا (they were) in the ayah.
The root letters ك-و-ن became past tense by adding fathas, and the middle و turned into ا
The وا shows this happened to a group of people (all male or mixed group) in the past
This is a preposition that connects words to show relationships
Added a fatha at the end to connect smoothly to the next word ٱلَّذِينَ (those who). This is like adding glue between words to make them flow better when speaking.
This is a relative pronoun used to describe people. It doesn't come from a root.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Adding hamza and alif at the start changes the meaning from 'trust' to 'believe'. This is a common pattern to make verbs stronger or more intense.

steps.m: they believed (subject of يَضْحَكُونَ - they laugh)
steps.n: Changed from 'he believed' to 'they believed' by adding وا at the end to show multiple people
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he laughed'
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action. In this ayah, it refers to the criminals (الَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا) mocking the believers (الَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا)
The verb has ون at the end to show multiple people (they) are doing the laughing

This is a word that introduces something that happened in time, like saying 'when' in English. It's often used to tell stories about things that happened or to describe what usually happens in certain situations.

steps.m: and when (connecting to what happened before)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this part with what was mentioned before. In this ayah, it connects to a previous description of what some people did.
Adding the fatha (َ) mark shows this is a past tense verb
Adding وا changes 'he' to 'they' (a group of people)
This is a preposition that shows how something is done or who it's done with. Here it connects to the verb يَتَغَامَزُونَ (they wink at) to show who they're winking at.
The هِمْ suffix means 'them' (masculine plural). In this ayah, it shows who is being winked at.

steps.m: to wink or signal with eyes

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
Adding alif shows two people winking back and forth at each other
Added تَ to show people doing the action to each other
Added يَ at the start to change from past tense (they winked) to present tense (he winks)
Added ونَ to show multiple people doing the action

steps.m: when (introduces the time they returned to their people)
steps.n: This is a word that shows when something happens. In this ayah, it introduces what these people did when they went back to their families.

steps.m: and when (connects to what happened before)
steps.n: The و connects this event to what happened before in the story. It shows this is part of a sequence of events.
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make a past tense verb
The prefix 'اِنْ' shows the action happens to the person doing it. Here it means they returned or turned themselves back.

steps.m: they turned themselves/returned (to أَهْلِهِمُ their families)
steps.n: The suffix 'وا' shows multiple people did this action. In this ayah, it refers to them returning to their families.
Basic preposition showing direction or destination
Added alif maksura (ىٰ) which is a spelling variation of the long 'aa' sound at the end of words
Added a small alif (ٓ) above to show connection to the next word أَهْلِهِمُ (their family). This is a pronunciation helper.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters. This changes the verb concept into a noun for the people who live together.
The suffix 'ِ' shows possession. The word أَهْلِ is possessed by the pronoun هِمُ (their), forming the meaning 'their family'.

steps.m: their family (referring to those who returned in انقَلَبُوٓا۟)
steps.n: The suffix 'هِمُ' shows possession by them (masculine plural). The final 'ُ' helps connect this word smoothly to the next word in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
The ان prefix shows the person did the action to themselves or returned to a previous state

steps.m: they turned themselves back (to أَهْلِهِمُ their families)
steps.n: The وا ending shows a group of people did the action. In this ayah, they are returning to their families (أَهْلِهِمُ)

steps.m: to be happy or enjoy oneself

steps.n: Changed from a verb (was happy) to an adjective (happy) that describes someone

steps.m: happy ones (describing a group of men)
steps.n: Added ين to show this describes many people who are happy
إِذَا is a conditional particle that introduces a situation that will happen in the future. It's used here because the verse is describing a scene that will occur.

steps.m: and when (followed by رَأَوْهُمْ - they saw them)
steps.n: Added وَ at the beginning to connect this clause with what came before it. This word introduces what happens when the disbelievers see the believers.
Connected the root letters and added fatha marks. The final ي changes to ى because it's a weak letter at the end.
Changed the final ى to وْ to show multiple people did the action. This is a special change because of the weak letter at the end.

steps.m: they saw them (the believers who they mock in this ayah)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show who was seen. In this ayah, it refers to the believers who the disbelievers are mocking.
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter
Adding وا makes it plural masculine - meaning more than one person did the action. In this ayah, the 'they' refers to some people talking about others they saw (هٰٓؤُلَآءِ/these people).
Adding the shadda (doubled letter) changes this from a conditional word to an emphasis word. It makes the next word هٰٓؤُلَآءِ (these people) take a special ending to show it's being emphasized.
This is the basic pointing word that shows something is near
Added ؤ to show we're pointing to multiple things
Added لَآ to emphasize we're pointing to multiple specific things

steps.m: these ones (who are being spoken about)
steps.n: Added ءِ at the end to point to specific people or things being discussed
Connected the root letters with fatha to form basic past tense verb
Active participle showing someone characterized by straying. This word is the predicate (khabar) of إنّ, telling us what the people are being called.
Makes the word plural to refer to multiple people who stray
The word was lengthened by adding ا (alif) to make it stronger/more emphatic, and ن (noon) was added at the end to show it's plural
Adding لَ (lam) at the start makes the meaning stronger - like saying 'surely' or 'indeed'
This is a negation particle that makes the verb after it negative, like adding 'not' in English
The و connects this phrase to what came before it, while the آ elongates the final sound for emphasis
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Changed from active (he sent) to passive (was sent) by adding أُ at the start

steps.m: they were sent (subject of the sentence)
steps.n: The وا shows that we're talking about a group of people (they)
This is the basic preposition showing something is on top of, upon, or over something else. It's one of the most common prepositions in Arabic.
The ى changes to يْ before adding pronouns at the end

steps.m: upon them (who were sent as mentioned in أُرْسِلُوا)
steps.n: Added هِمْ which means 'them' for a group of people. This refers back to those who were sent (أُرْسِلُوا) in the previous part of the verse.
This root is about keeping something safe or watching over it
Adding the vowels makes it a complete past tense verb meaning 'he protected'
Adding ا changes it from a verb (he protected) to a noun meaning someone who protects
Makes the word plural - more than one guardian
Adds fatha at the end to show the word's role in the sentence
Connected the root letters together with vowel marks to make the basic noun meaning 'day'
Adding 'ال' makes it refer to a specific day rather than any day
Added فَ to show this is happening as a result of something, and َ at the end to show this is when the laughing happens
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that refers to someone or something
Added ال with shadda to make it more specific, like saying 'the one' instead of just 'one'
Added ين at the end to make it plural. In this ayah, it refers to ٱلْكُفَّارِ (the disbelievers) who are laughing at the believers.
Added alif after first letter to change meaning from just feeling safe to actively believing
Added وا at the end to show this is about a group of men in the past
This is a basic preposition that shows something comes from, is part of, or belongs to something else. It's one of the most common Arabic prepositions.
Added fatha at the end because it connects to the next word ٱلْكُفَّارِ (the disbelievers). This helps make the words flow together smoothly when reading.
Added vowels to make it a past tense verb
Changed to plural noun form for people who disbelieve
Added definite article to specify these particular disbelievers
Added kasra at the end because it comes after the preposition مِنَ (from)
Connected the root letters together with vowels to make the past tense verb
Changes from past tense (he laughed) to present tense (he laughs) by adding يَ at the start

steps.m: they are laughing (referring to the disbelievers mentioned as ٱلْكُفَّارِ in the ayah)
steps.n: The suffix shows this is about a group. In this ayah, it refers to the disbelievers who are laughing at the believers.
This is a basic preposition that shows something is physically or metaphorically above something else
Adding ى at the end turns this into a preposition showing position. In this ayah, it shows people being positioned upon or on top of special couches/thrones in Paradise.
This is the basic concept of being settled comfortably
Added ي and ة to turn 'recline' into 'a single fancy couch for reclining'
Changed from single couch to many couches by adding ا after first letter and ئ in middle - like how 'chair' becomes 'chairs' but in Arabic
Added ال to make it specific

steps.m: on the decorated couches/thrones (in relation to عَلَى)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end because it follows the word عَلَى (on/upon)
This is the basic meaning of looking or observing
Added يَ at start to mean 'he' and show present tense, with ُ marks to complete the present tense pattern

steps.m: they are looking (referring to the disbelievers reclining on couches mentioned in عَلَى ٱلْأَرَآئِكِ)
steps.n: The ending ونَ shows this is about many people (3 or more men) doing the looking. In this ayah, it refers to the disbelievers who are reclining on couches (عَلَى ٱلْأَرَآئِكِ).
This is a question word that always comes at the start of a sentence to ask yes/no questions, like 'do' or 'have' in English questions
Added sukun mark to show end of word. In this ayah, it's asking 'Have the disbelievers been rewarded?'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
The doubled middle letter (shadda) makes the meaning stronger
Changed the vowels to show that someone received the action rather than did it. This word tells what happened to الْكُفَّارُ (the disbelievers) in this ayah.

steps.m: to reject, cover up, or hide truth

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
The shadda (doubled letter) shows intensity, and the alif after it makes it plural. Together they show people who regularly or strongly do this action.
Added ال to make it specific and ُ to show it's the subject doing the action. This word is the subject of ثُوِّبَ (were rewarded) in this ayah.

steps.m: what (referring to يَفْعَلُونَ - they used to do)
steps.n: This is a question word asking about the actions of the disbelievers. It connects with يَفْعَلُونَ (they used to do) to form the question 'what they used to do'

steps.m: to be or exist (in a state or condition)

steps.n: Added vowels (fatha) to make it past tense 'he was'
Added alif after ك because و is a weak letter that changes to alif in past tense
Added وا at the end to show multiple people did this action. This word is connected to يَفْعَلُونَ (they do) in the ayah, showing what they were doing.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: they do/are doing (referring to the disbelievers mentioned as الْكُفَّارُ)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people doing the action. This refers back to 'the disbelievers' (الْكُفَّارُ) mentioned earlier in the verse.
